documents.title: Scalability
documents.summary: Scalability is the ability of a system to handle more load by adding capacity without unacceptable cost, complexity, or reliability loss.
key_points: Horizontal scaling improves elasticity and fault isolation but introduces coordination and data-distribution costs.
Vertical scaling is simpler to reason about but eventually hits hardware, failover, and cost ceilings.
Scaling decisions should start from the actual bottleneck: compute, storage, network, coordination, or external dependency limits.
examples: Add stateless app replicas behind a load balancer when request volume grows.
Split read-heavy and write-heavy paths so each tier can scale independently.
pitfalls: Scaling one component while leaving the real bottleneck untouched.
Assuming more nodes automatically improve performance when contention dominates.
avoid_when: Avoid distributed complexity before you have measured where growth pressure actually lands.

documents.title: Load Balancing
documents.summary: Load balancing distributes traffic across healthy resources to improve utilization, availability, and operational flexibility.
key_points: Good health checks and connection handling usually matter more than the balancing algorithm itself.
Load balancers decouple clients from backend topology so instances can be replaced or scaled transparently.
Session affinity can simplify some flows, but it weakens even distribution and can create hot spots.
examples: Place multiple API replicas behind an ALB or reverse proxy tier.
Route traffic away from unhealthy instances during autoscaling or deployment.
pitfalls: Sending traffic to weak or partially failing instances because health checks are too shallow.
Using sticky sessions as a shortcut for state management.
avoid_when: Do not treat a load balancer as the solution to slow backends or poor capacity planning.

documents.title: Caching
documents.summary: Caching reduces latency and backend pressure by storing frequently reused data closer to the read path.
key_points: Pick the cache layer by scope: browser, CDN, application, or data tier.
The core tradeoff is freshness versus speed, so stale-read tolerance must be explicit.
A healthy miss path matters because caches fail, expire, and warm up under load.
examples: Use Redis for hot product reads with cache-aside loading.
Push semi-static assets and responses to a CDN to reduce origin load.
pitfalls: Caching without invalidation or TTL strategy.
Creating cache stampedes when many requests repopulate the same key together.
avoid_when: Avoid caching data whose correctness requirement is stricter than your invalidation model.

documents.title: Database Replication
documents.summary: Replication copies data across nodes to improve read scale, durability, and recovery posture, but it introduces lag and failover complexity.
key_points: Synchronous replication improves durability and consistency but increases write latency.
Asynchronous replication improves performance and distance tolerance but can expose stale reads or data loss windows.
Read replicas help read scale, not write bottlenecks.
examples: Use follower replicas for reporting or read-heavy traffic.
Replicate across zones or regions to support recovery goals.
pitfalls: Routing freshness-sensitive reads to lagging replicas.
Calling a replicated system highly available without validating failover behavior.
avoid_when: Avoid replica reads for workflows that require immediate read-after-write correctness.

documents.title: Sharding
documents.summary: Sharding spreads data across multiple nodes so storage and traffic scale beyond a single machine boundary.
key_points: Shard keys decide both scale quality and operational pain, so they must align with dominant access patterns.
Sharding improves capacity but increases cross-shard query, transaction, and rebalance complexity.
Uneven key distribution creates hot shards and defeats the point of partitioning.
examples: Partition tenants by customer id or user id for horizontal growth.
Spread writes across logical ranges or hashes to avoid a single primary bottleneck.
pitfalls: Choosing a shard key that is convenient for storage but terrible for traffic distribution.
Ignoring resharding cost until growth makes the first partitioning plan obsolete.
avoid_when: Avoid sharding before simpler options such as indexing, caching, or vertical scaling are exhausted.

documents.title: Queues and Pub/Sub
documents.summary: Queues decouple producers and consumers over time, while pub/sub decouples them across multiple subscribers.
key_points: Queues are for buffered work distribution and backpressure absorption.
Pub/sub is for fan-out when one event should reach multiple downstream systems independently.
Message ordering, retry behavior, and idempotency must be designed rather than assumed.
examples: Place order-processing work onto a queue so spikes do not overload the payment service.
Publish an account-created event to analytics, notifications, and CRM consumers.
pitfalls: Treating a queue like guaranteed business completion instead of work handoff.
Ignoring duplicate delivery and poison-message handling.
avoid_when: Avoid asynchronous messaging when the workflow requires immediate user-visible confirmation from every downstream step.

documents.title: Event-Driven Architecture
documents.summary: Event-driven architecture uses emitted facts about state changes to decouple producers from downstream reactions.
key_points: Events help scale independent consumers and reduce direct coupling between services.
The tradeoff is more eventual consistency, tracing complexity, and schema-governance responsibility.
Event contracts and ownership matter as much as the broker technology.
examples: Emit user-signup events that trigger onboarding, analytics, and fraud checks independently.
Publish inventory-updated events so read models and downstream projections refresh asynchronously.
pitfalls: Using events everywhere without clarifying which service owns the source-of-truth state.
Letting event schemas drift without versioning rules.
avoid_when: Avoid event-driven fan-out for simple workflows that are easier to understand as direct synchronous calls.

documents.title: Observability
documents.summary: Observability is the ability to infer system behavior from outputs such as metrics, logs, traces, and events.
key_points: Metrics show system health trends, logs explain local behavior, and traces connect end-to-end request flow.
Observability is essential because distributed failures often emerge from interactions rather than single-service errors.
The goal is actionable diagnosis, not maximum telemetry volume.
examples: Use traces to find which downstream call dominates latency.
Set SLO-aligned alerts on saturation, errors, and latency rather than infrastructure noise.
pitfalls: Collecting data without ownership, dashboards, or alert thresholds.
Relying only on logs in a distributed system where request flow spans many services.
avoid_when: Do not postpone observability until after launch if the system will be multi-service or high-volume.

documents.title: Zero Downtime Deployments
documents.summary: Zero-downtime deployment patterns reduce user disruption by shifting traffic gradually or safely between old and new versions.
key_points: Blue-green favors clean rollback by switching traffic between two environments.
Canary favors risk reduction by exposing a small percentage of traffic first and expanding when healthy.
Deployment safety depends on compatibility, health checks, and rollback signals, not just the routing pattern.
examples: Send 5 percent of traffic to the new build before full rollout.
Keep the old environment ready while validating the new one under production traffic.
pitfalls: Calling a rollout safe when database changes are not backward-compatible.
Skipping automated rollback conditions and relying on manual reaction time.
avoid_when: Avoid progressive rollout patterns without instrumentation strong enough to detect regressions quickly.

documents.title: SLO, SLA, and SLI
documents.summary: SLIs measure service behavior, SLOs define internal reliability targets, and SLAs define external commitments with business consequences.
key_points: SLIs should measure the user-facing behavior that actually matters, such as latency, availability, or freshness.
SLOs guide engineering tradeoffs and alerting thresholds before legal or contractual promises are involved.
Error budgets translate reliability targets into release and risk decisions.
examples: Track successful request rate and p95 latency as reliability indicators.
Pause risky releases when the service is burning through its error budget.
pitfalls: Choosing metrics that are easy to collect but disconnected from user experience.
Confusing an internal target with a contractual commitment.
avoid_when: Do not define reliability promises without instrumentation that can actually measure them.
